blocks.text: For the law of the Spirit of life in Christ Jesus has set you free from the law of sin and of death.

2 : , refers to the principle or ruling function of the Holy Spirit in the domain of Christ Jesus. The alternative in textual note is misleading, since Paul's focus in these verses is on God's Spirit (v. 9). , refers to the powers of sin and death which regulated humanity's existence until God delivered us; see 5.12–21; ch 6.
For God has done what the law, weakened by the flesh, could not do: by sending his own Son in the likeness of sinful flesh, and to deal with sin, he condemned sin in the flesh,

3 : , here the Jewish law, as at 2.12–15; 3.20–21; 7.14. , see 7.5. (lit., “in the likeness of the flesh of sin”), alludes to God's Son's complete identification with humanity's predicament, but without being identical to us; he is not enslaved to sin's power. See 2 Cor 5.21. , lit., “for the sake of, or concerning, sin”; the translation (textual note ) is a particular theological interpretation of the Gk. , see 7.5.
so that the just requirement of the law might be fulfilled in us, who walk not according to the flesh but according to the Spirit.a
8.1–4: What God has done for humanity.

4 : , see 7.12. , see 6.4. , opposing domains (see v. 6) regulated in the first instance by sin and death, and in the second by righteousness and life (see vv. 9–10). Being in the Spirit, which is another way Paul speaks of being in Christ Jesus (see 8.1), allows for the law to be fulfilled, whereas life in the flesh does not (see 7.5).
For those who live according to the flesh set their minds on the things of the flesh, but those who live according to the Spirita set their minds on the things of the Spirit.a
To set the mind on the flesh is death, but to set the mind on the Spirita is life and peace.
6 : See v. 4n.
For this reason the mind that is set on the flesh is hostile to God; it does not submit to God's law—indeed it cannot,
and those who are in the flesh cannot please God.
But you are not in the flesh; you are in the Spirit,a since the Spirit of God dwells in you. Anyone who does not have the Spirit of Christ does not belong to him.
But if Christ is in you, though the body is dead because of sin, the Spirit is life because of righteousness.

9–10 : , indicates the complete transformation offered in the domain of the Spirit; those in Christ Jesus both live in the Spirit and the Spirit lives in them. Paul can speak of the Spirit and interchangeably. The Spirit , that is, existence in and through God's Spirit means having resurrection life as a present promise (see 6.4n.) on account of the good news of the justifying power of God (see 1.16–17).
If the Spirit of him who raised Jesus from the dead dwells in you, he who raised Christ from the dead will give life to your mortal bodies also through his Spirit that dwells in you.
11 : , refers to the resurrection at Christ's return; see 5.10; 6.5,8; 1 Cor 15.12–24,50–57.
So then, brothers and sisters, we are debtors, not to the flesh, to live according to the flesh—
for if you live according to the flesh, you will die; but if by the Spirit you put to death the deeds of the body, you will live.

13 : , indicates the capacity available to those who live in Christ. See also 6.11–14. This God-given ability is in stark contrast to the predicament of those who live under the power of sin (see 1.18–32; 3.9–10). (lit., “practices”) refers to actions that result from the continuing existence and influence of sin, despite being in Christ/the Spirit.
For all who are led by the Spirit of God are children of God.
For you did not receive a spirit of slavery to fall back into fear, but you have received a spirit of adoption. When we cry, “Abba! Father!”
it is that very Spirit bearing witness with our spirit that we are children of God,

15–16 : , the Spirit does not make us slaves but adopted children; see also Gal 4.5–7. , this Spirit-prompted exclamation gives evidence with (the human spirit) that we are God's own children, in contrast to our previous hostility toward God (8.7; 5.10). is an Aramaic word which Jesus may have used in his own prayer (Mk 14.26).
and if children, then heirs, heirs of God and joint heirs with Christ—if, in fact, we suffer with him so that we may also be glorified with him.
8.5–17: Life in the Spirit.

17 : , see 8.39. , “if” can mean “seeing that,” indicating that this is in fact the case (see v. 18). Three words in this verse emphasize union with Christ: “joint heirs,” “suffer with,” and “glorified with.” See also 6.3–5.
I consider that the sufferings of this present time are not worth comparing with the glory about to be revealed to us.
For the creation waits with eager longing for the revealing of the children of God;

19 : , Paul personifies creation, and attaches the same verbs to creation and humanity, “wait” (vv. 19,23,25) and “groan” (v. 23). The inseparability of creation and humanity is emphasized. , see vv. 16–17,29.
for the creation was subjected to futility, not of its own will but by the will of the one who subjected it, in hope
that the creation itself will be set free from its bondage to decay and will obtain the freedom of the glory of the children of God.
20–21 : , another image for creation being in . This echoes Gen 3.17–19 where God curses the earth that Adam must nevertheless work.
We know that the whole creation has been groaning in labor pains until now;

22 : , Paul uses the image of labor pangs to describe the struggle involved in the transition from the present time to a glorious future. See also 1 Thess 5.3; Mk 13.8. This image also appears in Jewish apocalyptic literature (e.g., 62.).
and not only the creation, but we ourselves, who have the first fruits of the Spirit, groan inwardly while we wait for adoption, the redemption of our bodies.

23 : , the first part of the harvest that represents what is to come. See 8.11. , here “adoption” is future, in comparison to 8.15. , “redemption” means liberation (see 3.24n.). “Our bodies” may refer simply to “our selves,” or to the expectation that God will raise our bodies now dead because of sin to life that never ends. See 8.10–11.
For in hope we were saved. Now hope that is seen is not hope. For who hopes for what is seen?
But if we hope for what we do not see, we wait for it with patience.

8.18–25: Sufferings and glory. 18. , see 5.3–4. Being in Christ does not insulate believers from hardships. , probably another way Paul speaks of “the present evil age” (see Gal 1.4). See also 3.26. , see 5.2 and v. 17. See also Phil 3.21. , “reveal” translates the same Greek word used in 1.17 in relation to the gospel's revelation of the righteousness of God, and in 1.18 concerning God's wrath. This word appears also in v. 19.
Likewise the Spirit helps us in our weakness; for we do not know how to pray as we ought, but that very Spirit intercedes with sighs too deep for words.
26 : , here Paul introduces another benefit of life in the Spirit; see also 8.2–17. Some manuscripts include , possibly from a copyist clarifying Paul's meaning, see v. 27.
And God, who searches the heart, knows what is the mind of the Spirit, because the Spirit intercedes for the saints according to the will of God.
We know that all things work together for good for those who love God, who are called according to his purpose.
28 : , there are several manuscript variations of this verse; see textual note . , i.e., “chosen”; see 1.1,7. , namely humanity's conformity to Christ; see v. 29.
For those whom he foreknew he also predestined to be conformed to the image of his Son, in order that he might be the firstborn within a large family.

29 : , see Eph 1.4. Though from a human perspective God's intention that humans be conformed to the image of God's Son can be conceived as being prior in time, Paul affirms that there was never a moment when this was not God's will. , see v. 16–17; see also Col 1.15.
And those whom he predestined he also called; and those whom he called he also justified; and those whom he justified he also glorified.
8.26–30: Conformed to Christ's image.

30 : , is in the past tense, like “called” and “justified”; unlike v. 17, Paul sees temporality both from the human and divine perspective: though from our perspective being glorified is future, from God's perspective it has already occurred since it is God's will; see also v. 29n.
What then are we to say about these things? If God is for us, who is against us?
He who did not withhold his own Son, but gave him up for all of us, will he not with him also give us everything else?
32 : , lit., “spare”; see also 3.25; 4.25; 5.8; 8.3–4. , echoes Paul's concept of believers’ union with Christ, see also v. 17n.
Who will bring any charge against God's elect? It is God who justifies.
Who is to condemn? It is Christ Jesus, who died, yes, who was raised, who is at the right hand of God, who indeed intercedes for us.

34 : , Christ's resurrection is integrally connected to Christ's ascension to God's side. , see also v. 26 where it is the Spirit who intercedes. For Paul the activities of Christ and the Spirit are harmonious, see 8.9–10.
Who will separate us from the love of Christ? Will hardship, or distress, or persecution, or famine, or nakedness, or peril, or sword?
35 : , see v. 39 which speaks of God's love coming to us in Christ. See also 5.8. , the same Gk word is translated “sufferings” in 5.3.
As it is written, “For your sake we are being killed all day long; we are accounted as sheep to be slaughtered.”
No, in all these things we are more than conquerors through him who loved us.
For I am convinced that neither death, nor life, nor angels, nor rulers, nor things present, nor things to come, nor powers,
38 : , dying or living.
nor height, nor depth, nor anything else in all creation, will be able to separate us from the love of God in Christ Jesus our Lord.
8.31–39: The love of God in Christ Jesus.
39 : , can also be translated “creature” or “created thing.” The preceding list refers to things that are not God and includes also those not listed ( ) , see 5.5n.
